content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 16,
1994
Florence Rundell
City Clerk
City Hall, Third and H P0 130
Antioch,
CA 94509
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1—94—252
Dear Ms. Rundell:
We write in response to your request for advice regarding the
use of campaign account funds under the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) •1
OUESTION
May a mayor’s committee loan or contribute money to a
candidate for the Board of Directors of the Bay Area Rapid Transit
District
(“BART Board”)?
CONCLUS ION
Generally,
a candidate may not transfer campaign funds to any
candidate in a special election or to any candidate who is subject
to valid contribution limits.
If the BART Board candidate is not
running in a special election and is not subject to valid
contribution limits, the transfer of campaign funds,
by loan or by
contribution, would not be prohibited.
FACTS
The Mayor of Antioch wishes to use his campaign account to
contribute to a candidate for the Bart Board.
The City of Antioch
does not have local contribution limits.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—94—252
Page
2
ANALYSI S
Under Section 89513(g),
a candidate may loan campaign funds
to another candidate or committee unless prohibited by law.
Section 85304 prohibits the transfer of campaign funds between
candidates.
However,
in Service Employees International Union,
AFL—CIO,
CLC v.
Fair Political Practices Commission (9th Cir.
1992)
955 F.
2d 1312, cert.
den.
112
S.
Ct.
3056,
112 S.
Ct.
3057,
the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals invalidated the contribution
limits imposed by the Act,
and in so doing modified the scope of
Section 85304’s transfer prohibition.
Currently, transfers
between candidate dommittees are only prohibited when the
candidate who will receive the transfer is running in a special
election,
or if the candidate who will receive the transfer is
subject to local jurisdiction contribution limits.
Although you state that the City of Antioch does not have
local campaign contribution limits, it is not clear if the BART
candidate is subject to another jurisdiction with valid limits.
Accordingly, if the
BART candidate is running in a special
election or is subject to valid local election limits, the
transfer of funds would be prohibited.
Otherwise, the
loan or contribution of funds would not be prohibited under the
Act.
2
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Coun
2
The California Supreme Court has recently granted a petition
to review the contribution limit provisions of the Act to
determine whether principles of judicial construction would
eliminate the constitutional infirmity identified by the Ninth
Circuit Court of Appeals in Service Employees International Union,
supra.
Therefore, while candidate to candidate transfers,
as
outlined above,
do not currently violate the Act, the continued
validity of this advice is dependent on the outcome of the Supreme
Court’s review of the Act’s contribution limits.
(See Kopp v.
FPPC,
Cal.
Sup.
Ct.
Docket No.
SO38571.)
By:
Daniel
Muallem
Counsel,
Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a mayor’s committee loan or contribute money to a
candidate for the Board of Directors of the Bay Area Rapid Transit
District
(“BART Board”)?

CONCLUSION: Generally,
a candidate may not transfer campaign funds to any
candidate in a special election or to any candidate who is subject
to valid contribution limits.
If the BART Board candidate is not
running in a special election and is not subject to valid
contribution limits, the transfer of campaign funds,
by loan or by
contribution, would not be prohibited.

CONCLUS ION Generally, a candidate may not transfer campaign funds to any candidate in a special election or to any candidate who is subject to valid contribution limits. If the BART Board candidate is not running in a special election and is not subject to valid contribution limits, the transfer of campaign funds, by loan or by contribution, would not be prohibited. FACTS The Mayor of Antioch wishes to use his campaign account to contribute to a candidate for the Bart Board. The City of Antioch does not have local contribution limits. 1 Government Code Sections 81000—91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. Section 85304 prohibits the transfer of campaign funds between candidates. However, in Service Employees International Union, AFL—CIO, CLC v. Fair Political Practices Commission (9th Cir. 1992) 955 F. 2d 1312, cert. den. 112 S. Ct. 3056, 112 S. Ct. 3057, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals invalidated the contribution limits imposed by the Act, and in so doing modified the scope of Section 85304’s transfer prohibition. Currently, transfers between candidate dommittees are only prohibited when the candidate who will receive the transfer is running in a special election, or if the candidate who will receive the transfer is subject to local jurisdiction contribution limits. Although you state that the City of Antioch does not have local campaign contribution limits, it is not clear if the BART candidate is subject to another jurisdiction with valid limits. Accordingly, if the BART candidate is running in a special election or is subject to valid local election limits, the transfer of funds would be prohibited. Otherwise, the loan or contribution of funds would not be prohibited under the Act. 2 Sincerely, Steven G.
